title: Metrics
text: Pairwise distance functions for sequence comparison. All functions accept integer-encoded numpy arrays and return a scalar distance. Metric classes implement compute() (one pair) and pairwise() (full pool).

title: Metrics
text:
DistanceMatrix
@dataclass
class DistanceMatrix(values, labels=None)
Wrapper for a symmetric pairwise distance matrix.
Parameters








Name
Type
Description




values
np.ndarray
Square symmetric matrix of shape (n, n).


labels
list[int \| str] \| None
Sequence IDs corresponding to rows/columns.



Methods








Method
Returns
Description




to_condensed()
np.ndarray
Upper-triangle condensed form for scipy.


from_condensed(condensed, labels)
DistanceMatrix
Class method — build from condensed form.



Example
dist = pool.compute_distances("om", indel_cost=1.0)
print(dist.values.shape)   # (n, n)
print(dist.labels[:5])     # sequence IDs

title: Metrics
Metrics
text:
build_substitution_matrix
def build_substitution_matrix(
    n_states,
    method="constant",
    cost=2.0,
    transition_rates=None,
    state_frequencies=None,
) -> np.ndarray
Build a substitution cost matrix for use with Optimal Matching.



method
Description




"constant"
All substitutions cost cost (default 2.0).


"trate"
Costs derived from empirical transition rates.


"indels"
1/freq(a) + 1/freq(b) — requires state_frequencies.


"indelslog"
Log variant of "indels".


"future"
Chi-squared distance between next-state distributions.


"features"
Gower distance on user-defined state feature vectors.

title: Metrics
text:
Metric reference

Optimal Matching (OM)
def optimal_matching_distance(
    seq_a: np.ndarray,
    seq_b: np.ndarray,
    indel_cost: float = 1.0,
    substitution_costs: np.ndarray | None = None,
) -> float
Edit distance with custom insertion/deletion and substitution costs. The gold-standard metric for categorical sequence analysis (Abbott & Forrest 1986).
class OptimalMatchingMetric(indel_cost=1.0, substitution_costs=None)
    def compute(seq_a, seq_b) -> float
    def pairwise(pool) -> DistanceMatrix
Example
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric
from yasqat.statistics import substitution_cost_matrix

sub = substitution_cost_matrix(pool)          # data-driven costs
metric = OptimalMatchingMetric(substitution_costs=sub, indel_cost=1.0)
dist = metric.pairwise(pool)



Hamming
def hamming_distance(
    seq_a: np.ndarray,
    seq_b: np.ndarray,
    normalize: bool = False,
) -> float
Counts positions where sequences differ. Both sequences must have equal length. Numba-accelerated inner loop.
class HammingMetric(normalize=False)
    def compute(seq_a, seq_b) -> float
    def pairwise(pool) -> DistanceMatrix



Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)
def lcs_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
1 − LCS_length / max(len_a, len_b). Invariant to insertions/deletions.
class LCSMetric



Longest Common Prefix (LCP)
def lcp_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
Measures how early two sequences diverge. Sensitive to early-life differences.
class LCPMetric



Reversed LCP (RLCP)
def rlcp_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
LCP applied from the end — sensitive to late-trajectory differences.
class RLCPMetric



Dynamic Time Warping (DTW)
def dtw_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
Elastic alignment — stretches or compresses time to find the best match.
class DTWMetric



Soft-DTW
def softdtw_distance(
    seq_a: np.ndarray,
    seq_b: np.ndarray,
    gamma: float = 1.0,
) -> float
Differentiable relaxation of DTW (Cuturi & Blondel 2017). Useful as a smooth training loss.



Time-Warp Edit Distance (TWED)
def twed_distance(
    seq_a: np.ndarray,
    seq_b: np.ndarray,
    nu: float = 0.001,
    lmbda: float = 1.0,
) -> float
Combines edit distance with time-warping; nu controls stiffness, lmbda is the deletion cost.



Chi-squared (Chi2)
def chi2_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
Chi-squared distance between the state frequency distributions of two sequences. Order-invariant — treats sequences as bags of states.



Discrepancy-based Hamming Distance (DHD)
def dhd_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
Weighted Hamming distance accounting for state-level discrepancy.



Euclidean
def euclidean_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
Standard L2 norm between integer-encoded sequences.



OM Variants
def omloc_distance(seq_a, seq_b, indel_cost=1.0) -> float
def omspell_distance(seq_a, seq_b, indel_cost=1.0) -> float
def omstran_distance(seq_a, seq_b, indel_cost=1.0) -> float
OM extensions (Halpin 2010) that penalise state changes differently:

OMLoc — position-sensitive OM; earlier mismatches cost more.
OMSpell — spell-duration-sensitive OM.
OMStran — penalises transition-heavy sequences more strongly.




NMS family
def nms_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
def nmsmst_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
def svrspell_distance(seq_a: np.ndarray, seq_b: np.ndarray) -> float
Non-metric spell-based distances (Studer & Ritschard 2016):

NMS — number of matching subsequences.
NMSMst — NMS on the most frequent subsequence.
SVRSpell — Spell variant.

title: Clustering
text: Partitional and hierarchical clustering for sequences, plus quality indices and representative extraction. All clustering functions accept a DistanceMatrix (or its .values numpy array).

title: Clustering
text:
PAM — Partitioning Around Medoids
PAM (Kaufman & Rousseeuw 1990) is the standard algorithm for clustering distance matrices. It finds k medoid sequences that minimise total within-cluster dissimilarity.

pam_clustering
def pam_clustering(
    distance_matrix: np.ndarray,
    n_clusters: int,
    sequence_ids: list[int | str] | None = None,
    max_iter: int = 100,
    random_state: int | None = None,
) -> PAMClusteringResult
Parameters








Name
Type
Description




distance_matrix
np.ndarray
Square pairwise distance matrix (pass dist.values).


n_clusters
int
Number of clusters k.


sequence_ids
list \| None
Row/column labels. Defaults to integer indices.


max_iter
int
Maximum swap iterations.


random_state
int \| None
Seed for reproducible medoid initialisation.



Returns — PAMClusteringResult with attributes:








Attribute
Type
Description




labels
np.ndarray
Cluster assignment per sequence (0-indexed).


medoid_indices
np.ndarray
Index positions of medoid sequences.


cluster_sizes
dict[int, int]
Size of each cluster.



Methods on result








Method
Returns
Description




get_cluster_members(k)
list[int \| str]
Sequence IDs in cluster k.


get_medoid_ids()
list[int \| str]
Sequence IDs of medoids.


to_dataframe()
pl.DataFrame
Long-format table with sequence_id, cluster.



Example
from yasqat.clustering import pam_clustering

result = pam_clustering(
    distance_matrix=dist.values,
    n_clusters=4,
    sequence_ids=dist.labels,
    random_state=42,
)

print(result.cluster_sizes)
print(result.get_medoid_ids())


PAMClustering (class API)
class PAMClustering(n_clusters, max_iter=100, random_state=None)
    def fit(distance_matrix: np.ndarray, sequence_ids=None) -> PAMClustering
    @property labels -> np.ndarray | None
    @property medoid_indices -> np.ndarray | None
    @property result -> PAMClusteringResult | None
Scikit-learn-style class interface; equivalent to pam_clustering but reusable (call .fit() to train).

title: Clustering
text:
CLARA — Clustering Large Applications
CLARA (Kaufman & Rousseeuw 1990) scales PAM to large datasets by sampling. Repeatedly draws random subsets, runs PAM on each, keeps the best result.

clara_clustering
def clara_clustering(
    distance_matrix: np.ndarray,
    n_clusters: int,
    sequence_ids: list[int | str] | None = None,
    n_samples: int = 5,
    sample_size: int | None = None,
    max_iter: int = 100,
    random_state: int | None = None,
) -> PAMClusteringResult
Parameters









Name
Type
Default
Description




n_samples
int
5
Number of random sub-samples to try.


sample_size
int \| None
None
Size of each sub-sample. Defaults to 40 + 2k.



Returns the same PAMClusteringResult as pam_clustering.
from yasqat.clustering import clara_clustering

result = clara_clustering(
    distance_matrix=dist.values,
    n_clusters=5,
    sequence_ids=dist.labels,
    n_samples=10,
    random_state=42,
)

title: Clustering
text:
Quality metrics

pam_range
def pam_range(
    distance_matrix: DistanceMatrix,
    k_min: int = 2,
    k_max: int = 10,
    random_state: int | None = None,
) -> pl.DataFrame
Run PAM for each k in [k_min, k_max] and compute quality metrics. Returns a DataFrame with columns k, asw, hubert_c, total_cost.






Tip



Select k by maximising asw (Average Silhouette Width). An asw > 0.5 indicates a reasonable structure; > 0.7 is strong.


quality_df = pam_range(dist, k_min=2, k_max=8)
best_k = quality_df.filter(
    pl.col("asw") == pl.col("asw").max()
)["k"][0]


cluster_quality
def cluster_quality(
    distance_matrix: np.ndarray,
    labels: np.ndarray,
) -> pl.DataFrame
Compute quality metrics for a given clustering. Returns a one-row DataFrame:



Column
Description




asw
Average Silhouette Width (−1 to 1; higher is better).


hubert_c
Hubert’s C coefficient (0 to 1; lower is better).


total_cost
Sum of within-cluster distances to medoids.





silhouette_scores
def silhouette_scores(
    distance_matrix: np.ndarray,
    labels: np.ndarray,
) -> np.ndarray
Per-sequence silhouette values. Shape (n,).


silhouette_score
def silhouette_score(
    distance_matrix: np.ndarray,
    labels: np.ndarray,
) -> float
Mean silhouette width across all sequences (the asw).


distance_to_center
def distance_to_center(
    distance_matrix: np.ndarray,
    labels: np.ndarray,
) -> np.ndarray
Distance of each sequence to its cluster medoid. Shape (n,).

title: Clustering
text:
Representatives

extract_representatives
def extract_representatives(
    pool: SequencePool,
    distance_matrix: DistanceMatrix,
    labels: np.ndarray,
) -> dict[int, StateSequence]
Extract the medoid sequence for each cluster — the sequence that minimises total within-cluster dissimilarity.
Returns {cluster_id: StateSequence}.
reps = extract_representatives(pool, dist, labels=result.labels)

for cluster_id, seq in reps.items():
    print(f"Cluster {cluster_id} medoid: {seq.sequence_id}")
    print(seq.states[:10])

title: API Reference
text:
Quick imports
# Core
from yasqat import Alphabet, SequencePool
from yasqat.core.sequence import SequenceConfig

# IO — load from files or existing DataFrames
from yasqat.io import load_csv, load_parquet, load_dataframe

# Metrics
from yasqat.metrics import DistanceMatrix
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric
from yasqat.metrics.hamming import HammingMetric

# Statistics
from yasqat.statistics import (
    state_distribution, longitudinal_entropy, turbulence,
    transition_rate_matrix, badness, frequent_subsequences,
    discrepancy_analysis,
)

# Clustering
from yasqat.clustering import (
    pam_clustering, pam_range, cluster_quality, extract_representatives,
)

# Visualization
from yasqat.visualization import (
    index_plot, distribution_plot, entropy_plot,
    timeline_plot, parallel_coordinate_plot,
)

title: Changelog
text:
frequent_subsequences() now returns a pl.DataFrame with columns [subsequence, support, proportion] instead of a list of dataclass objects. The subsequence column is a list[str], enabling polars filtering like results.filter(pl.col("subsequence").list.len() == 2).
OptimalMatchingMetric is now exported from yasqat.metrics (previously only the function optimal_matching_distance was re-exported).





longitudinal_entropy() rewritten with polars group_by pipeline — replaces per-sequence Python loop and collections.Counter with a single-pass vectorized computation.
turbulence() rewritten with polars group_by over spell data — eliminates per-sequence DataFrame filtering.
normalized_turbulence() uses polars join + expressions instead of row-by-row iteration.





sunburst_plot() — concentric-ring visualisation showing hierarchical state transitions over time. Each ring represents a time position; arc widths are proportional to the number of sequences following each path.
tree_plot() — prefix-tree visualisation showing how sequences branch over time. Supports min_support pruning to focus on the most common paths.

OptimalMatchingMetric is now exported from yasqat.metrics (previously only the function optimal_matching_distance was re-exported).

title: Changelog
text:
0.3.1 (2026-03-09)

Performance

SequencePool._extract_sequences() replaced with O(N) group_by().agg() approach (was O(N×U) — one full table scan per unique ID). Significant speedup for large datasets (50k+ sequences).
SequencePool.describe() uses null-safe accessors for length statistics.



New features

load_dataframe() in yasqat.io — build a SequencePool directly from a polars DataFrame. Recommended entry point for Hive/Spark/Arrow workflows. Supports drop_nulls parameter for cleaning null states on ingestion.
transition_rates() and transition_rate_matrix() accept exclude_self parameter to filter out trivial self-transitions (A→A). Defaults to False.
All visualization functions (index_plot, distribution_plot, timeline_plot, spell_duration_plot) auto-suppress legends when the alphabet has >15 states. Pass show_legend=True to override.



Removals

TanaT source code removed (was already unused in v0.3.0).

title: Changelog
text:
0.3.0 (2026-02-23) — first public release

Breaking changes

SequencePool.compute_distances() now returns DistanceMatrix (with .values and .labels) instead of a raw np.ndarray.
sequence_ids is now a @property on all BaseSequence subclasses (StateSequence, EventSequence, IntervalSequence). Call it without parentheses: seq.sequence_ids not seq.sequence_ids().
IntervalSequence.to_state_sequence() now returns StateSequence instead of pl.DataFrame.



Bug fixes

Clustering functions (pam_clustering, clara_clustering, hierarchical_clustering) now correctly unwrap DistanceMatrix input via .values (previously checked for a non-existent .matrix attribute, silently passing the wrapper object through instead of the underlying array).
yasqat.__version__ now reads the installed version from importlib.metadata instead of a hardcoded string ("0.1.0") that disagreed with pyproject.toml ("0.2.1").



Additions

SequenceConfig is now exported from yasqat and yasqat.core.
Integration test suite added (tests/test_integration.py), covering the full pool → distances → clustering → quality workflow.

title: Descriptive Statistics
text: yasqat provides three families of statistics: descriptive measures of individual sequences, normative indicators that evaluate trajectories against a reference, and transition rates that characterise state-to-state dynamics.

title: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Setup
from yasqat.synthetic.financial import generate_customer_sequences
from yasqat.statistics import (
    state_distribution, longitudinal_entropy, turbulence,
    complexity_index, spell_count, visited_states,
)

df, alpha = generate_customer_sequences(n_customers=1_000, n_days=365, seed=42)
pool = SequencePool.from_dataframe(df, alphabet=alpha, id_col="customer_id")

title: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Descriptive statistics

State distribution
Proportion of sequences in each state at each time point. The workhorse for tracking cohort-level dynamics over time.
dist = state_distribution(pool)
# polars DataFrame: columns = states, rows = time points
# values = proportion [0, 1]
print(dist.head())


Longitudinal entropy
Shannon entropy computed across states at each time point. High entropy means the cohort is spread across many states; low entropy means most are in one state.
entropy = longitudinal_entropy(pool)
# polars DataFrame: time_point, entropy (float)


Turbulence
Per-sequence turbulence (Brzinsky-Fay 2007) — combines the number of distinct states visited with the variance in spell durations. Higher turbulence indicates more chaotic, unstable trajectories.
turb = turbulence(pool)
# polars DataFrame: sequence_id, turbulence (float)

# Find the most turbulent sequences
turb.sort("turbulence", descending=True).head(10)






Tip



Entropy vs. turbulence: entropy measures state diversity (how many different states), while turbulence measures spell dynamics (how often and how irregularly states change). A customer with high entropy but low turbulence visited many states but stayed in each for a long time. A customer with low entropy but high turbulence oscillated rapidly between a few states.




Complexity index
Normalised count of state transitions relative to sequence length.
complexity = complexity_index(pool)
# polars DataFrame: sequence_id, complexity (float in [0, 1])


Spell count and visited states
spells = spell_count(pool)
# polars DataFrame: sequence_id, n_spells (int)

visited = visited_states(pool)
# polars DataFrame: sequence_id, n_visited (int)


State duration statistics
Mean time and variance of time spent in each state per sequence:
from yasqat.statistics import mean_time_in_state, state_distribution

mean_time = mean_time_in_state(pool)
# polars DataFrame: sequence_id × state — mean spell duration

title: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Transition analysis

Transition rate matrix
The proportion of transitions from state i to state j across all sequences. Self-transitions (staying in the same state) are included by default.
from yasqat.statistics import transition_rate_matrix, transition_rates

# Full n_states × n_states matrix
rate_matrix = transition_rate_matrix(pool)

# Tidy long-format transition rates
rates = transition_rates(pool)
# polars DataFrame: from_state, to_state, rate, count
rates.filter(pl.col("from_state") != pl.col("to_state")).sort("rate", descending=True)
Use exclude_self=True to filter out trivial self-transitions (A→A) directly:
# Exclude self-transitions — cleaner than manual filtering
rates_no_self = transition_rates(pool, exclude_self=True)
rates_no_self.sort("count", descending=True).head(10)

# Also works on the matrix form
counts_no_self = transition_rate_matrix(pool, as_counts=True, exclude_self=True)


First occurrence time
At what time point does each sequence first enter each state?
from yasqat.statistics import first_occurrence_time

fot = first_occurrence_time(pool)
# polars DataFrame: sequence_id, state, first_t (int or null if never visited)


State duration statistics
from yasqat.statistics import state_duration_stats

dur = state_duration_stats(pool)
# polars DataFrame: state, mean_duration, std_duration, min_duration, max_duration

title: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Normative indicators
Normative indicators evaluate each sequence against a positive reference state (or set of states). These originated in labour-market research but apply to any domain where some states are “better” than others.






Note



These indicators follow TraMineR’s naming conventions exactly. If you know seqindic() in R, these are the Python equivalents.


from yasqat.statistics import (
    badness, insecurity, degradation,
    precarity, volatility, integration,
    proportion_positive,
)

# Define which states are "positive" (domain-specific)
positive_states = {"Employed", "Education"}







Indicator
What it measures




proportion_positive
Time spent in positive states / total time


badness
Weighted time in negative states (penalises duration)


insecurity
Time at risk of entering a negative state


degradation
Magnitude of downward moves away from positive states


precarity
Combined insecurity + instability


volatility
Frequency of up/down transitions across a threshold


integration
Sustained time in positive states (rewards continuity)



results = pl.concat([
    proportion_positive(pool, positive_states=positive_states),
    badness(pool, negative_states={"Unemployed", "Inactive"}),
    insecurity(pool, positive_states=positive_states),
], how="horizontal")

print(results.describe())

title: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Subsequence mining
Find patterns that appear frequently across sequences — useful for identifying dominant lifecycle arcs.
from yasqat.statistics import frequent_subsequences

patterns = frequent_subsequences(
    pool,
    min_support=0.1,   # must appear in at least 10 % of sequences
    max_length=3,      # up to 3-state patterns
)
# polars DataFrame: subsequence (list), support (float), count (int)

# Top 10 most common 2-step patterns
patterns.filter(pl.col("subsequence").list.len() == 2)\
        .sort("support", descending=True)\
        .head(10)

title: Visualization
text: All nine visualization functions return a plotnine.ggplot object — composable with + exactly like ggplot2 in R. No side effects: nothing is displayed until you call .draw() or save with .save().

title: Visualization
text:
Index plot
One row per sequence, one column per time step — coloured by state. The canonical sequence visualization. Best for small to medium pools (< 500 sequences).
p = index_plot(pool)
p.draw()

# Sorted by cluster assignment
p = index_plot(pool, group=cluster_labels, sort_by_group=True)
p + theme_bw() + labs(title="Trajectories by cluster")






Tip



sort_by_group=True stacks sequences by cluster, making within-cluster homogeneity immediately visible. Combine with group= to add a cluster boundary line between groups.

title: Visualization
text:
Distribution plot
State proportions at each time step — the cohort-level view. Stable bands mean long spells; thin bands mean transient states.
p = distribution_plot(pool)
p.draw()

# Proportion of sequences in each state over time
# Colour follows Alphabet.colors automatically

title: Visualization
text:
Entropy plot
Cross-sectional Shannon entropy at each time point. Peaks signal moments of maximum uncertainty in the cohort (e.g., at a policy change or economic shock).
p = entropy_plot(pool)
p + labs(y="Shannon entropy", x="Time point")

title: Visualization
text:
Frequency plot
The most common distinct sequences as horizontal bars. Useful for identifying whether a few dominant patterns account for most of the cohort.
p = frequency_plot(pool, n=20)   # top-20 most frequent sequences
p.draw()

title: Visualization
text:
Modal state plot
At each time point, the most common (modal) state. A smooth signal of the cohort’s “central tendency” trajectory.
p = modal_state_plot(pool)
p.draw()

title: Visualization
text:
Mean time plot
Mean time spent in each state, as a bar chart — one bar per state.
p = mean_time_plot(pool)
p + labs(title="Average time in state (all sequences)")

title: Visualization
text:
Timeline plot
Horizontal Gantt-style bars — one row per sequence, coloured bars per spell. Shows spell structure and duration directly.
# Subset to first 50 sequences for readability
subset = pool[:50]
p = timeline_plot(subset)
p.draw()

title: Visualization
text:
Spell duration plot
Distribution of spell lengths per state — how long do sequences stay in each state before transitioning? Useful for identifying “sticky” vs transient states.
p = spell_duration_plot(pool)
p + labs(x="Spell length (time steps)", y="State")

title: Visualization
text:
Parallel coordinate plot
One line per sequence — each axis is a time step, the line traces the state transitions. Works best with a coarse alphabet (≤ 6 states) and small pools.
p = parallel_coordinate_plot(pool, alpha=0.1)   # alpha for line transparency
p.draw()

title: Visualization
text:
Composing with plotnine
Every function returns a bare ggplot object. Apply any plotnine layer:
from plotnine import theme_bw, theme, element_text, labs, scale_fill_manual

p = index_plot(pool, group=cluster_labels)

p + theme_bw() \
  + theme(axis_text_y=element_text(size=7)) \
  + labs(title="Customer lifecycle trajectories", x="Day", y="Customer") \
  + scale_fill_manual(values=pool.alphabet.colors)

title: Visualization
text:
Legend auto-suppression
When the alphabet has more than 15 states, legends are automatically hidden to keep plots readable. All plot functions accept show_legend:
# Auto (default) — hidden when >15 states, shown otherwise
p = index_plot(pool)

# Force legend on, even with many states
p = index_plot(pool, show_legend=True)

# Force legend off, even with few states
p = index_plot(pool, show_legend=False)
This applies to index_plot, distribution_plot, timeline_plot, and spell_duration_plot.

title: Visualization
text:
Saving plots
p = index_plot(pool)
p.save("trajectories.png", dpi=150, width=12, height=8)
p.save("trajectories.pdf", width=12, height=8)






Note



plotnine uses matplotlib as its rendering backend. For interactive display in Jupyter, call p.draw(). In scripts, call p.save() or assign the output of p.draw() to a variable to suppress auto-display.

title: Distance Computation
text: Computing pairwise distances between sequences is the foundation for clustering, discrepancy analysis, and dissimilarity trees. Every metric in yasqat produces a DistanceMatrix — a typed numpy wrapper — that feeds directly into all downstream functions.

title: Distance Computation
text:
The DistanceMatrix
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric

dm = OptimalMatchingMetric().pairwise(pool)

print(dm)            # DistanceMatrix: 1000 × 1000
print(dm.values)     # numpy.ndarray, shape (1000, 1000), dtype float64
print(dm.labels)     # list of sequence IDs matching row/col order
print(dm.shape)      # (1000, 1000)

# Access a single pairwise distance
i, j = 0, 5
print(dm.values[i, j])






Note



DistanceMatrix wraps a symmetric numpy array where dm.values[i, i] == 0. Pass dm directly to clustering functions — they call .values internally.

title: Distance Computation
text:
Choosing a metric








Metric
Class
When to use




Optimal Matching
OptimalMatchingMetric
Gold standard for categorical sequences; handles insertions, deletions, substitutions with configurable costs. Social-science default.


Hamming
HammingMetric
Equal-length sequences, purely positional comparison. Fastest of all metrics.


LCS
LCSMetric
You care about shared subsequences, not timing. Good for variable-length sequences.


LCP
LCPMetric
Shared prefix matters most — early states are more informative than later ones.


RLCP
RLCPMetric
Shared suffix matters most — final states are most important.


DTW
DTWMetric
Elastic alignment needed; sequences may be “stretched” in time.


Chi-squared
Chi2Metric
Compare state-frequency histograms, ignoring order.

title: Distance Computation
text:
Optimal Matching
OM computes the minimum edit cost to transform one sequence into another through substitutions, insertions, and deletions.
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric
from yasqat.statistics import substitution_cost_matrix

# Default: constant substitution cost of 2, indel cost of 1
dm = OptimalMatchingMetric(indel_cost=1.0).pairwise(pool)

# Data-driven substitution costs from transition rates
sub_costs = substitution_cost_matrix(pool)
dm_custom = OptimalMatchingMetric(
    substitution_costs=sub_costs,
    indel_cost=1.0,
).pairwise(pool)






Tip



Data-driven substitution costs (computed from observed transition rates) are theoretically preferable to constant costs — states that commonly co-occur get lower substitution penalties. Use substitution_cost_matrix(pool) to compute them automatically.

title: Distance Computation
text:
Hamming
Position-wise mismatch count. Sequences must be the same length.
from yasqat.metrics.hamming import HammingMetric

dm = HammingMetric().pairwise(pool)
Hamming is the fastest metric in yasqat (numba-JIT inner loop). Use it when you have equal-length sequences and want a quick exploratory clustering.

title: Distance Computation
text:
LCS (Longest Common Subsequence)
Distance based on the length of the longest common subsequence: LCS_distance = 1 - (2 × LCS_length) / (len_a + len_b).
from yasqat.metrics.lcs import LCSMetric

dm = LCSMetric().pairwise(pool)

title: Distance Computation
text:
DTW (Dynamic Time Warping)
Elastic alignment that allows one sequence to be “stretched” to match another. Useful when temporal misalignment is expected (e.g., life events that occur at different ages across individuals).
from yasqat.metrics.dtw import DTWMetric

dm = DTWMetric().pairwise(pool)

title: Distance Computation
text:
Using a custom metric
All metrics subclass SequenceMetric from yasqat.metrics.base. To implement a custom metric:
from yasqat.metrics.base import SequenceMetric
from yasqat.core.metrics import DistanceMatrix

class MyMetric(SequenceMetric):
    def compute(self, seq_a, seq_b) -> float:
        # your pairwise distance logic
        ...

    def pairwise(self, pool) -> DistanceMatrix:
        # vectorised computation over the full pool
        ...

title: Quickstart
text: This guide walks from raw data to your first sequence plot in under 5 minutes. All code blocks show the API — no execution is required to follow along.

title: Quickstart
text:
1. Define an Alphabet
An Alphabet defines the valid states in your sequences plus colours and labels. Think of it as the legend for your entire analysis.
from yasqat import Alphabet

alpha = Alphabet(
    states=["Employed", "Unemployed", "Education", "Inactive"],
    colors=["#0f766e", "#dc2626", "#d97706", "#71717a"],
)
The alphabet is immutable (frozen=True dataclass) — it validates all sequences built from it. Invalid states raise a ValueError at construction time, not later.

title: Quickstart
text:
2. Load your sequences
SequencePool is the primary analysis unit. Pass a polars DataFrame in wide format — one column per time point, one row per individual.
import polars as pl
from yasqat import SequencePool

# Wide format: each column is one time step
df = pl.read_csv("sequences.csv")

pool = SequencePool.from_dataframe(
    df,
    alphabet=alpha,
    id_col="person_id",
    state_cols=["t1", "t2", "t3", "t4", "t5"],
)

print(len(pool))          # number of sequences
print(pool.length)        # sequence length (time points)
print(pool.sequence_ids)  # list of IDs

title: Quickstart
text:
3. Descriptive statistics
from yasqat.statistics import (
    state_distribution,
    turbulence,
    longitudinal_entropy,
)

# State proportions at each time point — returns polars DataFrame
dist = state_distribution(pool)

# Sequence turbulence (Brzinsky-Fay 2007)
turb = turbulence(pool)

# Shannon entropy per time point
entropy = longitudinal_entropy(pool)






Tip



All statistics functions return polars DataFrames — you can chain .filter(), .group_by(), .join() and the full polars API directly on the results.

title: Quickstart
text:
4. Compute pairwise distances
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric

metric = OptimalMatchingMetric(indel_cost=1.0)
dm = metric.pairwise(pool)

print(dm)           # DistanceMatrix: n × n
print(dm.values)    # underlying numpy array
See Distance Computation for a comparison of all seven metrics and guidance on which to use.

title: Quickstart
text:
5. Cluster sequences
from yasqat.clustering import pam_clustering, pam_range
from yasqat.clustering.quality import cluster_quality

# Find the right number of clusters
quality_df = pam_range(dm, k_min=2, k_max=8)

# Cluster with k=4
result = pam_clustering(dm, k=4)
print(result.labels)    # array of cluster assignments
print(result.medoids)   # indices of medoid sequences

title: Quickstart
text:
6. Visualise
from yasqat.visualization import index_plot, distribution_plot

# Sequence index plot coloured by cluster
p1 = index_plot(pool, group=result.labels)
p1.draw()

# State distribution over time
p2 = distribution_plot(pool)
p2.draw()
All plot functions return a plotnine.ggplot object — compose with + like ggplot2:
from plotnine import theme_bw, labs

p1 + theme_bw() + labs(title="Labour-market trajectories by cluster")






Tip



For a complete end-to-end walkthrough with a synthetic financial dataset, see the Full Demo.

Dependencies
yasqat pulls in the following packages automatically:



Package
Role




polars
DataFrames — never pandas


numpy
Numeric arrays and distance matrices

title: Data Structures
text: yasqat is built around three composable primitives: an Alphabet that defines the state space, sequences that encode individual trajectories, and a SequencePool that holds a collection of sequences for analysis.

title: Data Structures
text:
Alphabet
Alphabet is a frozen dataclass that defines the valid states for your analysis plus optional colours and labels. It is the shared vocabulary for every object you create downstream.
from yasqat import Alphabet

# Labour-market alphabet — ordered from most to least "positive"
alpha = Alphabet(
    states=[
        "Employed", "Education", "Inactive",
        "Unemployed", "Sick", "Unknown",
    ],
    colors=[
        "#0f766e", "#d97706", "#71717a",
        "#dc2626", "#7c3aed", "#a1a1aa",
    ],
)

print(alpha.states)   # ('Employed', 'Education', ...)
print(alpha.n_states) # 6






Tip



Colours are used automatically by every visualization function — define them once on the Alphabet and all plots are consistent.


Alphabets validate membership: passing an unknown state to any constructor raises ValueError immediately, not silently at render time.
# Raises ValueError: 'Retired' is not in the alphabet
from yasqat.core.sequence import StateSequence
StateSequence(states=["Employed", "Retired"], alphabet=alpha)

title: Data Structures
text:
Sequence types
yasqat supports three sequence representations, each suited to different data shapes.

StateSequence
Fixed-length categorical sequences — the most common type. One state per time step.
from yasqat.core.sequence import StateSequence, SequenceConfig

config = SequenceConfig(missing="Unknown", gap="Unknown")

seq = StateSequence(
    states=["Employed", "Employed", "Unemployed", "Education", "Employed"],
    alphabet=alpha,
    sequence_id="person_001",
    config=config,
)

print(seq.length)         # 5
print(seq.states)         # ('Employed', 'Employed', ...)
print(seq.spell_count())  # number of spells (runs of the same state)


EventSequence
Records events (state entries) at specific time points. Useful when only transitions are recorded, not the full state at every step.
from yasqat.core.sequence import EventSequence

events = EventSequence(
    events=[(0, "Employed"), (3, "Unemployed"), (5, "Education")],
    alphabet=alpha,
    sequence_id="person_002",
    total_length=12,
)


IntervalSequence
Spells with explicit start and end times. Natural fit for administrative records (job spells, benefit episodes) where duration is the primary unit.
from yasqat.core.sequence import IntervalSequence

spells = IntervalSequence(
    intervals=[(0, 6, "Employed"), (6, 9, "Unemployed"), (9, 12, "Education")],
    alphabet=alpha,
    sequence_id="person_003",
)

title: Data Structures
text:
SequencePool
SequencePool is the primary analysis unit — a collection of sequences with a shared alphabet. Most statistics and metric functions operate on a pool directly.

From a polars DataFrame (wide format)
import polars as pl
from yasqat import SequencePool

# Wide format: one column per time step
df = pl.DataFrame({
    "id": ["p001", "p002", "p003"],
    "t1": ["Employed", "Unemployed", "Education"],
    "t2": ["Employed", "Education",  "Employed"],
    "t3": ["Inactive", "Employed",   "Employed"],
})

pool = SequencePool.from_dataframe(
    df,
    alphabet=alpha,
    id_col="id",
    state_cols=["t1", "t2", "t3"],
)

print(len(pool))           # 3
print(pool.length)         # 3
print(pool.sequence_ids)   # ['p001', 'p002', 'p003']


From a long-format DataFrame
# Long format: one row per person-period
df_long = pl.read_csv("trajectories_long.csv")
# columns: person_id, year, state

pool = SequencePool.from_long_dataframe(
    df_long,
    alphabet=alpha,
    id_col="person_id",
    time_col="year",
    state_col="state",
)


Recoding states
When your raw data has more states than you want to analyse, use recode_states to map them onto a coarser alphabet before building the pool:
recode_map = {
    "full_time": "Employed",
    "part_time": "Employed",
    "job_seeker": "Unemployed",
    "benefit_only": "Unemployed",
    "in_school": "Education",
}

pool = SequencePool.from_dataframe(df, id_col="id", state_cols=state_cols)
pool = pool.recode_states(recode_map, new_alphabet=alpha)


Key properties
pool.n_sequences    # int — number of sequences
pool.length         # int — sequence length (all sequences share the same length)
pool.alphabet       # Alphabet — shared state space
pool.sequence_ids   # list[str] — IDs in insertion order

# Iterate over individual sequences
for seq in pool:
    print(seq.sequence_id, seq.states[:3])

title: Data Structures
text:
Trajectory and TrajectoryPool
Trajectory is a time-stamped variant of StateSequence for data where observations are irregularly spaced or carry calendar dates.
from yasqat import Trajectory, TrajectoryPool
import datetime as dt

traj = Trajectory(
    states=["Employed", "Unemployed", "Employed"],
    timestamps=[
        dt.date(2020, 1, 1),
        dt.date(2020, 6, 1),
        dt.date(2021, 1, 1),
    ],
    alphabet=alpha,
    trajectory_id="person_004",
)
TrajectoryPool collects trajectories exactly as SequencePool collects sequences. Most statistics functions accept either pool type interchangeably.






Note



Use SequencePool when all sequences have the same length and equal time spacing. Use TrajectoryPool when observations are dated or irregularly spaced.

title: Tutorials
text: These tutorials follow a single synthetic dataset — 1,000 financial customers tracked daily across 15 lifecycle states — from raw data through clustering and visualization. Each tutorial is self-contained; jump to any section.

title: Tutorials
text:
Dataset
All tutorials use a synthetic dataset generated by yasqat.synthetic:
from yasqat.synthetic.financial import generate_customer_sequences

df, alpha = generate_customer_sequences(
    n_customers=1000,
    n_days=365,
    seed=42,
)
This produces 1,000 customer trajectories across 15 lifecycle states (Active, Dormant, Churned, etc.) sampled from a Markov chain calibrated to approximate real financial customer behaviour.

title: Clustering
text: yasqat provides three clustering algorithms — PAM, CLARA, and hierarchical — all operating on a DistanceMatrix. Quality metrics (silhouette, pseudo-R²) and discrepancy analysis let you evaluate solutions and compare them across grouping variables.

title: Clustering
text:
Setup
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric
from yasqat.clustering import pam_clustering, pam_range, cluster_quality

dm = OptimalMatchingMetric(indel_cost=1.0).pairwise(pool)

title: Clustering
text:
Choosing the number of clusters
Run pam_range to compute Average Silhouette Width (ASW) across a range of k:
from yasqat.clustering import pam_range

quality_df = pam_range(dm, k_min=2, k_max=8)
# polars DataFrame: k, asw, n_clusters

print(quality_df)
best_k = quality_df.filter(
    pl.col("asw") == pl.col("asw").max()
)["k"][0]
print(f"Best k by ASW: {best_k}")






Tip



ASW values above 0.5 indicate a reasonable structure; above 0.7 is strong. For sequence data in social science, values of 0.2–0.4 are common and still interpretable — the goal is meaningful typologies, not tight clusters.

title: Clustering
text:
PAM (Partitioning Around Medoids)
PAM finds k representative sequences (medoids) that minimise total distance to all sequences in their cluster.
from yasqat.clustering import pam_clustering

result = pam_clustering(dm, k=best_k)

print(result.labels)    # array of int cluster assignments, shape (n,)
print(result.medoids)   # list of medoid sequence indices
print(result.cost)      # total within-cluster distance (PAM objective)

# Add cluster labels back to your DataFrame
df_labelled = df.with_columns(
    pl.Series("cluster", result.labels)
)

Extract representative sequences
from yasqat.clustering import extract_representatives

reps = extract_representatives(pool, dm, labels=result.labels)
# dict: cluster_id → StateSequence (the medoid sequence)

for cluster_id, medoid_seq in reps.items():
    print(f"Cluster {cluster_id}: {medoid_seq.states[:5]}...")

title: Clustering
text:
CLARA (Clustering Large Applications)
CLARA is a sampling-based extension of PAM — suitable when N > 500. It repeatedly draws random subsamples, runs PAM on each, and keeps the solution with the lowest total cost.
from yasqat.clustering import clara_clustering

result = clara_clustering(
    dm,
    k=best_k,
    n_samples=5,        # number of subsamples
    sample_size=200,    # size of each subsample
    seed=42,
)

print(result.labels)
print(result.cost)






Note



CLARA trades exactness for speed. For datasets under ~500 sequences, use PAM directly. For thousands of sequences, CLARA is the practical choice.

title: Clustering
text:
Hierarchical clustering
Ward-linkage hierarchical clustering via scipy. Returns a HierarchicalClustering object with a linkage matrix for dendrogram inspection.
from yasqat.clustering import hierarchical_clustering

hc = hierarchical_clustering(dm, method="ward")

# Cut the dendrogram at k clusters
labels = hc.cut(k=best_k)
print(labels)

# Access the raw scipy linkage matrix
print(hc.linkage.shape)   # (n-1, 4)

title: Clustering
text:
Quality metrics

Silhouette score
Average Silhouette Width — the primary quality index. Values range from −1 (wrong cluster) to +1 (perfect separation).
from yasqat.clustering.quality import silhouette_score, silhouette_scores

# Scalar ASW for the whole solution
asw = silhouette_score(dm, labels=result.labels)
print(f"ASW = {asw:.3f}")

# Per-sequence silhouette values
per_seq = silhouette_scores(dm, labels=result.labels)
# polars DataFrame: sequence_id, cluster, silhouette


Cluster quality summary
from yasqat.clustering.quality import cluster_quality

cq = cluster_quality(dm, labels=result.labels)
# polars DataFrame: cluster, n, asw, mean_distance, max_distance
print(cq)

title: Clustering
text:
Discrepancy analysis
Discrepancy analysis (pseudo-R²) measures how much of the total sequence variation is explained by group membership — analogous to ANOVA for sequences.
from yasqat.statistics import discrepancy_analysis, multi_factor_discrepancy

# How well do clusters explain sequence variation?
disc = discrepancy_analysis(dm, groups=result.labels)
print(f"Pseudo-R² = {disc.pseudo_r2:.3f}")
# Values above 0.1 indicate meaningful group differences

# Test multiple grouping variables
multi_disc = multi_factor_discrepancy(
    dm,
    factors={
        "cluster": result.labels,
        "gender": df["gender"].to_list(),
        "age_group": df["age_group"].to_list(),
    },
)
print(multi_disc)
# polars DataFrame: factor, pseudo_r2, p_value (permutation test)






Tip



Use discrepancy analysis to validate that your clusters are not just artifacts of a demographic covariate. If gender alone explains as much variance as cluster, your clustering may be capturing gender differences rather than trajectory types.

title: Sequence analysisfor social science.
text:
A modern Python library for categorical sequence analysis. Built on polars and plotnine — no pandas, no matplotlib API. Designed for life-course and social-science researchers.


Get Started → API Reference



from yasqat import Alphabet, SequencePool
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric
from yasqat.clustering import pam_clustering
from yasqat.visualization import index_plot

# Define labour-market states
alpha = Alphabet(
    states=["Employed", "Unemployed", "Education", "Inactive"],
    colors=["#0f766e", "#dc2626", "#d97706", "#71717a"],
)

# Build a pool from your data
pool = SequencePool.from_dataframe(df, alphabet=alpha)

# Compute pairwise distances
dm = OptimalMatchingMetric().pairwise(pool)

# Cluster and visualise
labels = pam_clustering(dm, k=4).labels
index_plot(pool, group=labels).draw()





Polars-native


Fast by design
No pandas dependency. All data operations run through polars — columnar, lazy, and fast even on large panel datasets with thousands of sequences.
# Returns a polars DataFrame
pool.state_distribution()




Distance metrics


Seven metrics included
Optimal Matching, Hamming, LCS, DTW, and more. Every metric outputs a DistanceMatrix — a typed numpy wrapper compatible with all clustering functions.
dm = HammingMetric().pairwise(pool)
dm.values   # numpy ndarray, shape (n, n)




TraMineR-compatible


Algorithms you already know
Algorithm correctness cross-referenced against TraMineR (R) and TanaT (Python). Naming conventions follow TraMineR so the transition from R is immediate.
turbulence(pool)        # Brzinsky-Fay
discrepancy_analysis()  # pseudo-R²




Visualization


Nine plot types, one grammar
Index plots, timelines, modal states, parallel coordinates — all return composable plotnine.ggplot objects. Compose with + exactly like ggplot2 in R.
p = index_plot(pool)
p + theme_bw()   # fully composable

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text: A complete end-to-end walkthrough of the yasqat workflow using a synthetic financial dataset. 1,000 customers are tracked daily across 15 lifecycle states for one year (365 days).

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 0: Imports
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from plotnine import theme_bw, labs, theme, element_text

from yasqat import Alphabet, SequencePool
from yasqat.core.sequence import SequenceConfig
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric
from yasqat.metrics.hamming import HammingMetric
from yasqat.clustering import (
    pam_clustering, clara_clustering, hierarchical_clustering,
    pam_range, cluster_quality, extract_representatives,
)
from yasqat.statistics import (
    state_distribution, longitudinal_entropy, turbulence,
    complexity_index, spell_count, visited_states,
    transition_rate_matrix, transition_rates,
    badness, insecurity, degradation, precarity, volatility,
    frequent_subsequences, discrepancy_analysis,
)
from yasqat.visualization import (
    index_plot, timeline_plot, spell_duration_plot,
    distribution_plot, entropy_plot, frequency_plot,
    modal_state_plot, mean_time_plot, parallel_coordinate_plot,
)
from yasqat.synthetic.financial import generate_customer_sequences

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 1: Synthetic Data Generation
The synthetic generator produces realistic financial customer journeys using a calibrated Markov chain. States are collapsed from 35 granular raw states into 15 lifecycle stages.
# Generate 1,000 customers × 365 days across 15 lifecycle states
raw_df, _ = generate_customer_sequences(
    n_customers=1_000,
    n_days=365,
    seed=42,
)

print(f"Shape: {raw_df.shape}")    # (365000, 3)
print(raw_df.head(10))
The 15 lifecycle states span the full customer journey:



Category
States




Acquisition
prospect, applying


Onboarding
product_applied, new_member


Activity
dormant, low_activity, active, power_user


Product depth
product_expand


Digital
digital


Financial health
healthy, at_risk, delinquent, default


Exit
churned



# State recode map: 35 raw states → 15 lifecycle stages
RECODE_MAP = {
    "lead": "prospect",
    "application_started": "applying",
    # ... (see source for full mapping)
}

ORDERED_STATES = [
    "prospect", "applying", "product_applied", "new_member",
    "dormant", "low_activity", "active", "power_user",
    "product_expand", "digital", "healthy",
    "at_risk", "delinquent", "default", "churned",
]

STATE_COLORS = [
    "#c8d8e4", "#a8c4d4", "#88b0c4", "#5a9ab5",
    "#d4c5b0", "#c9a882", "#e8b84b", "#f0a500",
    "#8ab89a", "#5a9a7a", "#2d7a55",
    "#e88a5a", "#d45a2a", "#b02020", "#601010",
]

# Build pool: raw → recode → custom alphabet
pool_raw = SequencePool(raw_df.select(["id", "time", "state"]))
pool_recoded = pool_raw.recode_states(RECODE_MAP)

alphabet = Alphabet(states=ORDERED_STATES, colors=STATE_COLORS)
pool = pool_recoded.with_alphabet(alphabet)

print(f"{len(pool)} sequences, {pool.length} time points")

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 2: Core Data Structures
# Access a single sequence
seq = pool[0]
print(seq.sequence_id)
print(seq.states[:10])
print(seq.spell_count())

# Pool metadata
print(pool.alphabet.states)
print(pool.sequence_ids[:5])

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 3: Descriptive Statistics
# State distribution at each time point
dist = state_distribution(pool)
print(dist)

# Sequence-level entropy and turbulence
ent = longitudinal_entropy(pool)
turb = turbulence(pool)

# Joined summary
summary = ent.join(turb, on="sequence_id")
print(summary.describe())

# Spell structure
spells = spell_count(pool)
visited = visited_states(pool)

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 4: Transition Analysis
# Full transition rate matrix
rate_matrix = transition_rate_matrix(pool)
print(rate_matrix)

# Tidy transition rates — top 10 most common transitions
rates = transition_rates(pool)
rates.filter(pl.col("from_state") != pl.col("to_state"))\
     .sort("rate", descending=True)\
     .head(10)

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 5: Normative Indicators
# Positive states in this domain = financial health
positive = {"healthy", "active", "power_user", "digital"}
negative = {"at_risk", "delinquent", "default", "churned"}

prop_pos  = proportion_positive(pool, positive_states=positive)
bad       = badness(pool, negative_states=negative)
insec     = insecurity(pool, positive_states=positive)
prec      = precarity(pool, positive_states=positive)
vol       = volatility(pool, positive_states=positive)

results = pl.concat([prop_pos, bad, insec, prec, vol], how="horizontal")
print(results.describe())

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 6: Frequent Subsequence Mining
patterns = frequent_subsequences(
    pool,
    min_support=0.05,   # in at least 5 % of sequences
    max_length=3,
)

# Top 10 two-step patterns
patterns.filter(pl.col("subsequence").list.len() == 2)\
        .sort("support", descending=True)\
        .head(10)

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 7: Distance Computation
# Data-driven substitution costs from transition rates
from yasqat.statistics import substitution_cost_matrix
sub_costs = substitution_cost_matrix(pool)

# Optimal Matching with data-driven costs
metric = OptimalMatchingMetric(substitution_costs=sub_costs, indel_cost=1.0)
dist_matrix = metric.pairwise(pool)

print(dist_matrix)
print(f"Min: {dist_matrix.values.min():.2f}")
print(f"Max: {dist_matrix.values.max():.2f}")
print(f"Mean: {dist_matrix.values.mean():.2f}")

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 8: Clustering
# Select k by ASW
quality_df = pam_range(dist_matrix, k_min=2, k_max=8)
best_k = quality_df.filter(
    pl.col("asw") == pl.col("asw").max()
)["k"][0]
print(f"Best k: {best_k}  (ASW = {quality_df['asw'].max():.3f})")

# PAM clustering
pam_result = pam_clustering(
    distance_matrix=dist_matrix.values,
    n_clusters=best_k,
    sequence_ids=dist_matrix.labels,
)

print(f"Cluster sizes: {pl.Series(pam_result.labels).value_counts()}")

# Extract medoid sequences
reps = extract_representatives(pool, dist_matrix, labels=pam_result.labels)

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 9: Discrepancy Analysis
disc = discrepancy_analysis(dist_matrix, groups=pam_result.labels)
print(f"Pseudo-R² = {disc.pseudo_r2:.3f}")
print(f"F-statistic = {disc.f_stat:.3f}")
print(f"p-value = {disc.p_value:.4f}")

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 10: Built-in Visualizations
# 1. Distribution plot — state proportions over time
p1 = distribution_plot(pool)
p1 + labs(title="State distribution over 365 days")

# 2. Index plot — sorted by cluster
p2 = index_plot(pool, group=pam_result.labels, sort_by_group=True)
p2 + theme_bw() + labs(title=f"Trajectories ({best_k} clusters)")

# 3. Entropy plot
p3 = entropy_plot(pool)
p3 + labs(title="Cross-sectional entropy over time")

# 4. Frequency plot — top 20 most common sequences
p4 = frequency_plot(pool, n=20)
p4.draw()

# 5. Modal state plot
p5 = modal_state_plot(pool)
p5.draw()

# 6. Mean time per state
p6 = mean_time_plot(pool)
p6.draw()

# 7. Timeline plot (first 50 sequences)
p7 = timeline_plot(pool[:50])
p7.draw()

# 8. Spell duration distribution
p8 = spell_duration_plot(pool)
p8.draw()

# 9. Parallel coordinates (first 100 sequences)
p9 = parallel_coordinate_plot(pool[:100], alpha=0.05)
p9.draw()

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 11: Custom Visualizations
plotnine’s composability lets you build arbitrarily complex plots from yasqat outputs. A few examples:
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes, geom_tile, geom_point,
    scale_fill_gradient2, facet_wrap,
    element_blank,
)

# Entropy vs turbulence scatter coloured by cluster
scatter_df = longitudinal_entropy(pool)\
    .join(turbulence(pool), on="sequence_id")\
    .with_columns(pl.Series("cluster", pam_result.labels))

p = (
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes("entropy", "turbulence", color="factor(cluster)"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.4, size=1.2)
    + theme_bw()
    + labs(
        title="Entropy vs turbulence by cluster",
        x="Longitudinal entropy",
        y="Turbulence (Brzinsky-Fay)",
        color="Cluster",
    )
)
p.draw()

title: Full Demo — Customer Lifecycle Analytics
text:
Section 12: Summary
print("=== yasqat Demo Summary ===")
print(f"  Sequences:        {len(pool):,}")
print(f"  Time points:      {pool.length}")
print(f"  States:           {pool.alphabet.n_states}")
print(f"  Best k (PAM):     {best_k}")
print(f"  Pseudo-R²:        {disc.pseudo_r2:.3f}")

title: Visualization
text: Nine plotnine-based functions for sequence visualization. Every function returns a bare plotnine.ggplot object — composable with + exactly like ggplot2 in R. Nothing is rendered until you call .draw() or .save().

title: Visualization
text:
index_plot
def index_plot(
    pool: SequencePool,
    group: np.ndarray | list[int] | None = None,
    sort_by_group: bool = False,
) -> ggplot
One row per sequence, one column per time step — coloured by state. The canonical sequence visualization (sequence index plot, SIP).
Parameters








Name
Type
Description




pool
SequencePool
The sequence pool to plot.


group
array-like \| None
Cluster labels; enables colour-coded group boundaries.


sort_by_group
bool
If True, stacks sequences by cluster assignment.



p = index_plot(pool)
p.draw()

# Sorted by cluster, with theme overrides
p = index_plot(pool, group=cluster_labels, sort_by_group=True)
p + theme_bw() + labs(title="Trajectories by cluster", x="Day", y="Customer")






Tip



Best for small to medium pools (< 500 sequences). For large pools, use distribution_plot or subsample first with pool.sample(200).

title: Visualization
text:
distribution_plot
def distribution_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
Stacked area chart showing the proportion of sequences in each state at each time point. Cohort-level view; uses Alphabet.colors automatically.
p = distribution_plot(pool)
p + labs(title="State distribution over time", x="Day", y="Proportion")

title: Visualization
text:
entropy_plot
def entropy_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
Cross-sectional Shannon entropy at each time point. Peaks signal moments of maximum cohort diversity (e.g. after a policy shock).
p = entropy_plot(pool)
p + labs(y="Shannon entropy", x="Time point")

title: Visualization
text:
frequency_plot
def frequency_plot(pool: SequencePool, n: int = 10) -> ggplot
Horizontal bar chart of the n most common distinct sequences. Useful for detecting dominant patterns.
Parameters



Name
Default
Description




n
10
Number of top sequences to display.



p = frequency_plot(pool, n=20)
p.draw()

title: Visualization
text:
modal_state_plot
def modal_state_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
At each time point, shows the most common (modal) state. Provides the “central tendency” trajectory of the cohort.
p = modal_state_plot(pool)
p.draw()

title: Visualization
text:
mean_time_plot
def mean_time_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
Horizontal bar chart — one bar per state, showing mean time steps spent in that state across all sequences.
p = mean_time_plot(pool)
p + labs(title="Average time in state")

title: Visualization
text:
timeline_plot
def timeline_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
Horizontal Gantt-style chart — one row per sequence, coloured bars representing each spell’s state and duration.
# Subset for readability
subset = pool[:50]
p = timeline_plot(subset)
p.draw()

title: Visualization
text:
spell_duration_plot
def spell_duration_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
Distribution of spell lengths per state (density or box plots). Reveals which states are “sticky” vs. transient.
p = spell_duration_plot(pool)
p + labs(x="Spell length (time steps)", y="State")

title: Visualization
text:
parallel_coordinate_plot
def parallel_coordinate_plot(
    pool: SequencePool,
    alpha: float = 0.1,
) -> ggplot
One line per sequence — each axis is a time step, the line traces state transitions. Best with a coarse alphabet (≤ 6 states) and small pools.
Parameters








Name
Default
Description




alpha
0.1
Line transparency. Lower values reduce overplotting.



p = parallel_coordinate_plot(pool[:100], alpha=0.05)
p.draw()

title: Visualization
text:
Composing with plotnine
Every function returns a bare ggplot. Apply any plotnine layer with +:
from plotnine import theme_bw, theme, element_text, labs, scale_fill_manual

p = index_plot(pool, group=cluster_labels)

p + theme_bw() \
  + theme(axis_text_y=element_text(size=7)) \
  + labs(title="Customer lifecycle trajectories", x="Day", y="Customer") \
  + scale_fill_manual(values=pool.alphabet.colors)

title: Visualization
text:
Saving plots
p = index_plot(pool)
p.save("trajectories.png", dpi=150, width=12, height=8)
p.save("trajectories.pdf", width=12, height=8)






Note



plotnine uses matplotlib as its rendering backend. In Jupyter, call p.draw(). In scripts, call p.save() or assign the result of p.draw() to a variable to suppress auto-display.

title: IO
text:
Hive / Spark integration
For data originating from Hive tables or Spark jobs, the recommended workflow is:

Export from Spark to Parquet (cloud storage or Unity Catalog Volume)
Read with pl.read_parquet() or pl.scan_parquet().collect()
Pass to load_dataframe() with a SequenceConfig mapping your columns

from yasqat.io import load_dataframe
from yasqat.core.sequence import SequenceConfig

df = pl.read_parquet("s3://bucket/events_export/")
pool = load_dataframe(
    df,
    config=SequenceConfig(
        id_column="user_id",
        time_column="event_ts",
        state_column="event_type",
    ),
    drop_nulls=True,
)






Tip



For small-to-medium tables, use PySpark’s .toArrow() + pl.from_arrow() for a zero-copy bridge that avoids writing intermediate files.

title: Statistics
text: Descriptive statistics, normative indicators, transition analysis, and subsequence mining. All functions accept SequencePool and return pl.DataFrame.

title: Statistics
text:
Descriptive statistics
All functions take pool: SequencePool and return a pl.DataFrame with at minimum a sequence_id column (one row per sequence) or a time column (one row per time step).

state_distribution
def state_distribution(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Proportion of sequences in each state at each time point. Returns a long-format DataFrame with columns time, state, proportion.
dist = state_distribution(pool)
# time | state   | proportion
# 0    | active  | 0.312
# 0    | dormant | 0.185


longitudinal_entropy
def longitudinal_entropy(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Shannon entropy of the state distribution at each time step, then averaged across time per sequence. Returns sequence_id, entropy.


turbulence
def turbulence(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Brzinsky-Fay (2007) turbulence — combines variance of spell durations with the number of distinct states visited. Returns sequence_id, turbulence.


normalized_turbulence
def normalized_turbulence(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Turbulence normalised to [0, 1] range across the pool.


complexity_index
def complexity_index(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Billari (2001) complexity index — weighted combination of spell count and number of distinct states. Returns sequence_id, complexity.


spell_count
def spell_count(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Number of distinct consecutive spells per sequence. Returns sequence_id, spell_count.


visited_states
def visited_states(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Number of distinct states visited per sequence. Returns sequence_id, visited_states.


visited_proportion
def visited_proportion(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
visited_states / total_states — fraction of the alphabet visited. Returns sequence_id, visited_proportion.


mean_time_in_state
def mean_time_in_state(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Average time steps spent in each state, aggregated across all sequences. Returns state, mean_time.


modal_states
def modal_states(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
The most common state at each time point. Returns time, modal_state, proportion.


sequence_length
def sequence_length(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Number of time steps per sequence. Returns sequence_id, length.


sequence_frequency_table
def sequence_frequency_table(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Unique sequences with their frequencies and relative frequencies. Useful for identifying dominant patterns. Returns sequence, count, proportion.


transition_count
def transition_count(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Number of state transitions per sequence. Returns sequence_id, transition_count.


transition_proportion
def transition_proportion(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Transitions per time step — normalised transition count. Returns sequence_id, transition_proportion.


subsequence_count
def subsequence_count(
    pool: SequencePool,
    subsequence: list[str],
) -> pl.DataFrame
Count occurrences of a specific subsequence in each sequence. Returns sequence_id, count.


sequence_log_probability
def sequence_log_probability(
    pool: SequencePool,
    transition_matrix: np.ndarray | None = None,
) -> pl.DataFrame
Log probability of each sequence under a (optionally provided) first-order Markov model. Returns sequence_id, log_probability.

title: Statistics
text:
Transition analysis

transition_rate_matrix
def transition_rate_matrix(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Empirical first-order transition rates between all state pairs. Returns a wide-format DataFrame (states × states).
rate_matrix = transition_rate_matrix(pool)


transition_rates
def transition_rates(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Long-format tidy transition rates. Returns from_state, to_state, rate.
rates = transition_rates(pool)
rates.filter(pl.col("from_state") != pl.col("to_state"))\
     .sort("rate", descending=True)\
     .head(10)


substitution_cost_matrix
def substitution_cost_matrix(pool: SequencePool) -> np.ndarray
Data-driven substitution costs derived from empirical transition rates (TraMineR’s seqsubm "TRATE" method). Returns a (n_states, n_states) numpy array ready for OptimalMatchingMetric.


first_occurrence_time
def first_occurrence_time(
    pool: SequencePool,
    state: str,
) -> pl.DataFrame
Time step at which each sequence first enters a given state. Returns sequence_id, first_occurrence (null if never entered).


state_duration_stats
def state_duration_stats(pool: SequencePool) -> pl.DataFrame
Mean, median, min, max spell duration per state. Returns state, mean_duration, median_duration, min_duration, max_duration.

title: Statistics
Statistics
text:
Discrepancy analysis

discrepancy_analysis
def discrepancy_analysis(
    distance_matrix: DistanceMatrix,
    groups: np.ndarray | list[int],
) -> DiscrepancyResult
ANOVA-like test for whether group membership explains sequence dissimilarity (Studer et al. 2011). Returns a DiscrepancyResult with:








Attribute
Type
Description




pseudo_r2
float
Proportion of total dissimilarity explained by groups.


f_stat
float
Pseudo-F statistic.


p_value
float
Permutation-based p-value.



disc = discrepancy_analysis(dist_matrix, groups=pam_result.labels)
print(f"Pseudo-R² = {disc.pseudo_r2:.3f}")
print(f"p-value   = {disc.p_value:.4f}")


multi_factor_discrepancy
def multi_factor_discrepancy(
    distance_matrix: DistanceMatrix,
    factors: dict[str, np.ndarray],
) -> pl.DataFrame
Discrepancy analysis for multiple grouping factors simultaneously. Returns a DataFrame with one row per factor: factor, pseudo_r2, f_stat, p_value.

title: Statistics
text:
Subsequence mining

frequent_subsequences
def frequent_subsequences(
    pool: SequencePool,
    min_support: float = 0.1,
    max_length: int = 5,
) -> pl.DataFrame
Mine frequent state subsequences (PrefixSpan algorithm). Returns subsequence (list), support (proportion of sequences containing it).
patterns = frequent_subsequences(pool, min_support=0.05, max_length=3)
patterns.filter(pl.col("subsequence").list.len() == 2)\
        .sort("support", descending=True)\
        .head(10)

title: Statistics
text:
Dissimilarity tree

dissimilarity_tree
def dissimilarity_tree(
    distance_matrix: DistanceMatrix,
    groups: np.ndarray | None = None,
    max_depth: int = 5,
    min_group_size: int = 10,
) -> DissimilarityTree
CART-like recursive splitting on a distance matrix. At each node, finds the binary partition that maximises between-group dissimilarity (pseudo-R²). Useful for understanding the hierarchical structure of sequence variation.
